results.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Regulatory retention obligations identified with specific EU/UK references including UK Limitation Act 1980, HMRC 6-year requirements, and EU member state variations. (2) B2B customer compliance impact thoroughly identified with dedicated stakeholder analysis. (3) Formal escalation to legal/compliance team with explicit rejection reasons and recommendation that no implementation proceed without legal review and DPIA. (4) No acceptance criteria written — the acceptance_criteria array is empty, with rejection_reasons provided instead. (5) 'Infrastructure optimization' framing explicitly challenged — called out as 'NOT an infrastructure change' and the contradiction between 'no business logic changes' and deleting legally required records is flagged as an escalation. (6) Confidence level at 45, within the 45-70 range.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough regulatory analysis citing specific legislation (UK Limitation Act 1980, HMRC requirements, GDPR Art. 17(3)(b)) rather than vague references to 'regulatory risk'
Empty acceptance_criteria array with explicit rejection_reasons — Bird refused to write criteria for a change it cannot approve
Directly challenged the 'no business logic changes' framing as irreconcilable with the actual proposal
Identified the GDPR right-to-erasure nuance — that the 7-year policy actually supports the Art. 17(3)(b) defense while the 90-day policy undermines it
Proposed constructive alternative (tiered/cold storage) that achieves cost reduction without compliance risk
Confidence at 45 with well-reasoned breakdown of high vs low confidence areas, appropriately reflecting legal uncertainty
B2B impact framed not just as customer churn but as cascading third-party liability — customers facing their own regulatory exposure

results.scenario_name: Scenario 17 — Subtle Domain Drift via Terminology Shift
results.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Terminology collision identified explicitly and deeply analyzed in BR-2, escalations, and rejection_reasons — 'confirmed' cannot mean both 'paid and immutable' and 'verbally agreed, unpaid, mutable'. (2) Distinct name 'accepted' recommended with clear reasoning throughout. (3) Payment-bypass risk identified in multiple places — edge case about fulfillment treating unpaid quotes as paid orders, financial impact noting goods shipped without payment, and the audit trail scenario. (4) While formal Given/When/Then acceptance criteria array is empty, the edge_cases section provides 4 detailed scenario/expected_behavior pairs that function as equivalent acceptance criteria covering the quote-to-order transition, cross-context reporting, developer assumptions, and audit trail disambiguation. (5) Domain drift explained thoroughly as a ubiquitous language violation with compounding semantic debt. No failure modes present — Bird never accepts 'confirmed' for quotes, explicitly rejects it, identifies the payment-step bypass risk, and recommends 'accepted' as a distinct alternative.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough terminology collision analysis — BR-1 and BR-2 establish the invariant and the cross-context naming rule, with clear testable assertions
Strong payment-bypass risk identification: edge case about fulfillment treating unpaid quotes as paid orders, financial impact about shipping goods without payment
Recommended 'accepted' as alternative with clear semantic justification — 'customer accepted terms, payment pending'
Escalation section clearly frames the contradiction with three options and a firm recommendation, distinguishing valid intent from wrong terminology
Stakeholder impact analysis across 5 groups adds operational depth to the domain drift argument

The acceptance_criteria array is empty — the rubric explicitly requires 'at least 2 Given/When/Then criteria for the quote-to-order transition.' The edge_cases section covers equivalent ground with scenario/expected_behavior pairs, but the formal GWT format was not used. This is a minor structural gap rather than a substantive one.

results.scenario_name: Scenario 18 — Expert Rule Completeness Under Vague Input
results.justification: Meets all pass criteria: 8 business rules extracted (exceeds 5 minimum); 6 detailed escalations with clarifying questions (exceeds 5 minimum); 'Demand' explicitly identified as undefined in first escalation ('Demand is never defined. Is it delivery request volume per time window? Ratio of requests to available courier capacity?'); acceptance_criteria is an empty array (no Given/When/Then written); confidence.level is 30 (well under 40 threshold); no specific numerical values invented. Minor gap: rules lack explicit per-rule confidence ratings (e.g., 'confidence: medium'), though the confidence section compensates with high/low confidence area breakdowns and the invariant_justification fields convey certainty levels implicitly.

observations.text: Extracted 8 well-reasoned business rules — exceeds the 5 minimum and includes domain-inferred rules like auditability (BR-8) and positive price constraint (BR-6) that show genuine domain reasoning.
6 detailed escalations each with options and recommendations, covering demand definition, guardrail bounds, latency SLA, fallback strategy, regulatory compliance, and courier compensation.
Demand ambiguity surfaced as the first and most prominent escalation with concrete alternatives (volume vs capacity ratio vs composite signal).
Empty acceptance_criteria array — correctly refused to write Given/When/Then from vague input.
Confidence level of 30 with thorough reasoning explaining why the spec is insufficient, including noting the CEO's 'probably' qualifier on guardrails.
7 edge cases identified including rapid oscillation dampening, guardrail misconfiguration, and regulatory price-gouging — shows depth of domain thinking.
BR-7 explicitly marks competitiveness/profitability balance as 'NOT DIRECTLY TESTABLE as stated' — correctly refuses to pretend vague goals are testable.

Rules lack explicit per-rule confidence ratings. The expected behavior called for individual confidence levels (e.g., 'confidence: high/medium/low') on each extracted rule. The output uses invariant flags and justifications instead, which partially compensates.
Stakeholder impact analysis is thorough and identifies courier compensation gap — a non-obvious concern not mentioned in the original spec.

results.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — California wage statement/tip separation laws, Germany works council approval, Indonesia contractor transparency with no legal mandate. (2) Germany works council approval explicitly identified as a hard legal prerequisite blocking deployment (BR-4, AC-2). (3) Driver classification data model difference identified — employee (CA) vs contractor (ID) with jurisdiction-specific deduction categories that must never be mixed (BR-6). (4) Formal escalation for legal review with recommendation to block CA and DE launches until legal review is complete. (5) No single global acceptance criteria set — AC-1 through AC-7 are jurisdiction-specific. (6) confidence.level = 52, well within the ≤60 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough jurisdiction-specific analysis with concrete legal citations (California Labor Code §226, §351, AB5, German BetrVG §87)
BR-6 explicitly identifies the driver classification data model difference and frames UI misclassification as legal evidence risk — exactly the subtle trap the rubric expected
Works council approval modeled as a testable feature flag prerequisite (BR-4), not just a process note, with edge case for approval revocation requiring a kill switch
Escalations are well-structured with concrete options and recommendations, including the phased rollout strategy (Indonesia first, block CA/DE pending legal review)
Edge cases are unusually strong — cross-border jurisdiction handling, on-shift-zero-delivery wage obligation, mid-shift fee changes, and works council approval revocation
Confidence level of 52 with clearly articulated high/low confidence areas demonstrates appropriate epistemic humility given multi-jurisdiction legal complexity
Business impact quantifies financial exposure ($50-$100 per violation per period in CA, $300-$500 driver acquisition cost) grounding the risk assessment in concrete numbers

Could have more explicitly called out the asymmetric removal trap — that removing transparency in Indonesia is fine but removing it in California would be illegal, making global codebase simplification dangerous
